name: Effect v4 Pattern: README - Pattern Guide Overview
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Effect Library Development Patterns Overview
Category: pattern
Source: README.md

Description: Overview of the Effect library development patterns directory, establishing the purpose, available patterns, and critical principles for development.

When to Use: Reference when starting new work on Effect v4 codebase, onboarding new contributors, or conducting code reviews.

Key Points:
- Patterns directory serves as authoritative guide for Effect library development
- Core patterns: effect-library-development (fundamentals), module-organization (structure/exports/naming), error-handling (structured error management)
- Documentation patterns: jsdoc-documentation (standards and examples)
- Testing patterns: testing-patterns (@effect/vitest and TestClock strategies)
- Architecture patterns: platform-integration (service abstractions, cross-platform)

Forbidden Patterns (NEVER USE):
- try-catch in Effect.gen: breaks Effect's error handling semantics
- Type assertions: `as any`, `as never`, `as unknown` hide type errors
- Unsafe patterns: anything that bypasses Effect's type safety

Mandatory Patterns (ALWAYS USE):
- `return yield*` for errors: makes termination explicit in generators
- Immediate linting: `pnpm lint --fix` after editing TypeScript files
- JSDoc compilation validation: `pnpm docgen` must pass
- Effect testing patterns: use @effect/vitest with `it.effect`
- TestClock for time: use TestClock for any time-dependent operations

Related Patterns: All patterns in the .patterns/ directory

name: Effect v4 Pattern: Effect Library Development - Core Patterns
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Effect Library Development Fundamentals
Category: pattern
Source: effect-library-development.md

Description: Fundamental patterns for developing high-quality, type-safe code within the Effect library ecosystem, covering Effect.gen composition, function constructors, error handling, resource management, and layer composition.

When to Use: When writing any Effect-based code, choosing between Effect.gen/fn/fnUntraced, implementing error handling, managing resources, or composing layers.

Key Points:
- CRITICAL FORBIDDEN: Never use try-catch in Effect.gen (breaks Effect semantics). Use Effect.result instead.
- CRITICAL FORBIDDEN: Never use type assertions (as any, as never, as unknown). Fix underlying type issues.
- MANDATORY: Always use `return yield*` when yielding terminal effects (Effect.fail, Effect.interrupt). Missing return leads to unreachable code.

Effect.gen vs Effect.fn vs Effect.fnUntraced:
- Effect.gen: One-off effect composition, inline operations, no function wrapper needed
- Effect.fn: Reusable effectful functions for public API, creates traced span + stack trace. Supports piping transformations after function body.
- Effect.fnUntraced: Internal library implementations, performance-critical hot paths, no tracing overhead

Example (Effect.fn with piped transformations):
```typescript
const fetchWithTimeout = Effect.fn("fetchWithTimeout")(
  function*(url: string) {
    return yield* Effect.tryPromise(() => fetch(url))
  },
  Effect.timeout("5 seconds"),
  Effect.retry({ times: 3 })
)
```

Error Handling: Use Data.TaggedError for structured, typed errors with _tag discrimination.
Resource Management: Use Effect.acquireUseRelease for automatic resource cleanup.
Layer Composition: Build applications using layered architecture with Context.Tag service interfaces.

Development Workflow:
- Immediate linting after editing TypeScript files
- Validation checkpoint: lint, docgen, check, test, build
- Progressive implementation: break complex features into validated increments

Related Patterns: error-handling.md, testing-patterns.md, module-organization.md

name: Effect v4 Pattern: Error Handling
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Error Handling in Effect
Category: pattern
Source: error-handling.md

Description: Comprehensive error handling patterns for the Effect library, covering structured error types, error creation, error handling combinators, testing, and recovery strategies.

When to Use: When defining errors, handling errors in Effect pipelines, testing error conditions, or implementing retry/fallback logic.

Key Points:

Three Error Type Patterns:
1. Data.TaggedError: Core pattern with _tag for catchTag discrimination. Use for most application errors.
2. Data.Error: Simpler pattern without _tag. Use when discrimination not needed.
3. Schema.ErrorClass: For serializable, schema-validated errors in CLI, HTTP APIs, distributed systems. Pattern: `Schema.ErrorClass('TypeId/Name')({ _tag: Schema.tag('Name'), fields... })`

Error Composition:
- Use flat union types for composition, NOT abstract base classes
- Better type inference, simpler catchTag, serialization-friendly
- Pattern: `type HttpClientError = RequestError | ResponseError`

Error Creation:
- Effect.try: For operations that might throw synchronously
- Effect.tryPromise: For Promise-based operations
- Always wrap with structured error types in the catch handler

Error Handling Combinators:
- Effect.catchAll: Handle all errors uniformly
- Effect.catchTag: Handle specific tagged error types
- Effect.catchSome: Selectively handle certain errors (return Option)

Error Testing:
- Use Effect.exit to inspect success/failure without throwing
- Test with catchTag to verify error handling paths
- Use Exit.isFailure/Exit.isSuccess for assertions

Error Recovery Patterns:
- Retry with exponential backoff: Schedule.exponential with Schedule.whileInput and Schedule.recurs
- Fallback chain: acc.pipe(Effect.orElse(() => fallback))

Standardized Error Reasons:
- SystemErrorReason: AlreadyExists, BadResource, Busy, InvalidData, NotFound, PermissionDenied, TimedOut, UnexpectedEof, Unknown, WouldBlock, WriteZero
- HttpClientErrorReason: Transport, Encode, InvalidUrl, StatusCode, Decode, EmptyBody

Related Patterns: effect-library-development.md, platform-integration.md

name: Effect v4 Pattern: JSDoc Documentation
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: JSDoc Documentation Standards
Category: pattern
Source: jsdoc-documentation.md

Description: Comprehensive JSDoc documentation patterns for the Effect library, ensuring consistent, practical, and compilable examples for all APIs.

When to Use: When documenting any Effect library API, writing JSDoc examples, adding category annotations, or fixing docgen errors.

Key Points:

Critical Requirements:
- MANDATORY: All JSDoc examples must compile via `pnpm docgen`
- ZERO TOLERANCE: Even pre-existing docgen errors must be fixed
- FORBIDDEN: Removing examples to fix compilation - always fix type issues properly
- FORBIDDEN: `any` types, type assertions, `declare` patterns in examples

Import Standards:
- Core Effect: `import { Array, Effect, Console } from "effect"`
- Schema: `import { Effect, Schema } from "effect"` (NOT from "@effect/schema" - deprecated)
- Canonical: `import * as S from "effect/Schema"`; WRONG: `import { Schema } from "effect/schema"`

Standard JSDoc Structure:
1. Brief one-line description
2. Detailed explanation if needed
3. @example blocks with working, compilable code
4. @since version annotation
5. @category annotation (lowercase)

Category Names (lowercase):
- constructors, combinators, transforming, utilities, predicates, getters, models, symbols, guards, refinements, mapping, filtering, folding, sequencing, error handling, resource management, concurrency, testing, interop, elements, math, mutations

Example Patterns:
- Constructor examples: show different type arguments
- Combinator examples: show both data-first and data-last (pipeable) usage
- Effect pattern examples: use Effect.gen with yield*
- Error handling examples: use Data.TaggedError, Effect.fail, catchTag

Related Patterns: module-organization.md, effect-library-development.md

name: Effect v4 Pattern: Module Organization
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Module Organization and Structure
Category: pattern
Source: module-organization.md

Description: Established patterns for organizing modules in the Effect library, covering directory structure, file naming, exports, TypeId patterns, dual functions, variance annotations, and import conventions.

When to Use: When creating new modules, organizing exports, implementing TypeId patterns, creating dual functions, or structuring imports.

Key Points:

Directory Structure:
- Flat module structure: core modules are top-level .ts files (Array.ts, Effect.ts, Schema.ts, etc.)
- packages/effect/src/internal/: Private implementation details
- packages/effect/src/testing/: Test utilities (FastCheck, TestClock, TestConsole, TestSchema)
- packages/effect/src/unstable/: Experimental features (ai/, cli/, cluster/, http/, httpapi/, rpc/, sql/, workers/, workflow/)

Export Pattern:
- Main index.ts uses flat namespace exports: `export * as Array from "./Array.ts"`
- Uses .ts extensions in imports (NOT .js)
- Barrel exports are auto-generated with `// @barrel:` comment

TypeId Pattern (String Literals, NOT Symbol.for):
- Format: `const TypeId = "~effect/ModuleName"`
- Nested: `"~effect/Queue/Dequeue"`, `"~effect/platform/FileSystem"`
- Versioned: `export const EffectTypeId = \`~effect/Effect/${version}\``
- Type guard: `export const isX = (u: unknown): u is X => Predicate.hasProperty(u, TypeId)`

Dual Function Pattern:
- `export const map = dual<DataLastSig, DataFirstSig>(2, implementation)`
- Supports both data-first `map(array, fn)` and data-last `pipe(array, map(fn))` usage
- Arity-based: number for fixed params, predicate function for variable

Naming Conventions:
- Constructors: make, of, empty, fromIterable
- Combinators: map, flatMap, filter, zip
- Predicates: isSome, isNone, isEffect
- Destructors: getOrElse, match, toArray
- Types: PascalCase (Effect, Option, Either)
- TypeLambda suffix for type lambdas
- NonEmpty prefix for non-empty variants

Pipeable Integration:
- Interfaces extend Pipeable and Inspectable.Inspectable
- Prototype pattern with PipeInspectableProto from internal/core.ts

Import Organization: External > Effect modules (alphabetical, .ts) > Utilities > Internal > Type-only

Related Patterns: effect-library-development.md, jsdoc-documentation.md

name: Effect v4 Pattern: Platform Integration
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Platform Integration and Service Abstractions
Category: pattern
Source: platform-integration.md

Description: Patterns for integrating platform-specific functionality within the Effect ecosystem, including service abstractions, layer compositions, cross-platform compatibility, stream integration, and resource management.

When to Use: When implementing platform-specific services (FileSystem, HTTP, Sockets), converting Node.js streams to Effect streams, managing resources with Scope, or testing with mock services.

Key Points:

Service Abstraction Pattern:
- Define platform-agnostic interfaces in core Effect package
- FileSystem uses ServiceMap.Service: `export const FileSystem: ServiceMap.Service<FileSystem, FileSystem> = ServiceMap.Service("effect/platform/FileSystem")`
- Tagged service pattern: `class HttpPlatform extends ServiceMap.Service<HttpPlatform, {...}>()(...)`

Node.js Implementation:
- Use `effectify` to convert Node.js callback APIs to Effect
- `handleErrnoException` maps errno codes to SystemErrorReason (ENOENT->NotFound, EACCES->PermissionDenied, EEXIST->AlreadyExists)
- `handleBadArgument` handles synchronous argument validation errors
- Layer export: `Layer.succeed(FileSystem.FileSystem, make())`

Stream Integration:
- Node.js Readable to Effect Stream: `Stream.fromChannel(fromReadableChannel(options))` using `Channel.fromTransform`
- Bun ReadableStream: optimized with `.readMany()` API
- Effect Stream to Node.js Readable: `toReadable` wraps in StreamAdapter

Layer Composition:
- Service aggregation: `Layer.mergeAll(NodeFileSystem.layer, NodePath.layer)`
- Test layers: use `Layer.succeed(FileSystem.FileSystem, mockFileSystem)` for mocking
- `Layer.fresh` for fresh layer instances in tests

Resource Management:
- Scoped resource acquisition using `Effect.scope` + `Scope.addFinalizer`
- HTTP server: ensure server.close() on scope close
- Connection pool pattern with Pool.make and Pool.get

Error Handling:
- Platform errors transformed via standardized SystemErrorReason enum
- HTTP-specific errors via HttpErrorReason enum

Bun Platform: Currently reuses Node.js FileSystem implementation from @effect/platform-node-shared

Related Patterns: error-handling.md, effect-library-development.md

name: Effect v4 Pattern: Testing Patterns
episode_body:
Title: Pattern: Testing with @effect/vitest and TestClock
Category: pattern
Source: testing-patterns.md

Description: Comprehensive testing strategies for the Effect library using @effect/vitest, with emphasis on proper Effect patterns, TestClock usage, service mocking, and type-safe assertions.

When to Use: When writing any tests for Effect-based code, testing time-dependent operations, mocking services, or asserting on Effect results.

Key Points:

Framework Selection:
- @effect/vitest for Effect-based tests: `import { assert, describe, it } from "@effect/vitest"`
- Regular vitest for pure TypeScript functions only

FORBIDDEN:
- Never use Effect.runSync in tests with regular `it` - use `it.effect` instead
- Never use `expect` with `it.effect` - use `assert` methods instead

TestClock (CRITICAL for time-dependent tests):
- `TestClock.adjust("5 seconds")`: advance time (preferred string duration pattern)
- `TestClock.setTime(1000)`: set absolute time
- Required for: Effect.sleep, Effect.delay, Effect.timeout, Effect.race, scheduled operations, retry logic, queue operations with time-based completion
- Pattern: fork operation, adjust TestClock, join fiber

Assertion Patterns:
- `assert.strictEqual(actual, expected)`: reference/primitive equality
- `assert.deepStrictEqual(actual, expected)`: deep structural equality
- `assert.deepEqual(actual, expected)`: uses Equal.equals trait for Effect types
- `assert.isTrue(condition)`, `assert.isFalse(condition)`
- Exit assertions: `assertExitSuccess(result, value)`, `assertExitFailure(result, error)` from `@effect/vitest/utils`
- Result assertions: `assertSuccess(result, value)`, `assertFailure(result, error)`

Error Testing Pattern:
- Use `Effect.exit(operation)` to capture success/failure without throwing
- Check `result._tag === "Failure"` for failure cases
- Use Exit.isFailure/Exit.isSuccess

Service Mocking:
- ServiceMap.Service pattern for defining services
- `Layer.succeed(Service)({ method: () => Effect.succeed(value) })` for simple mocks
- Static Mock/Failing factories on test service classes

Test Organization:
- Group by: constructors, combinators, predicates, error handling, integration
- Progressive complexity: basic -> configured -> error handling -> concurrency -> full integration

Related Patterns: effect-library-development.md, error-handling.md
